comments:
Summary:
The paper proposes Collaborative Deep Embedding (DualNet), a pair of jointly trained deep encoding networks for items and users to predict user–item matchings. Item encoders operate on item content (e.g. text or visual features) while user encoders take a user’s history (a sparse vector over reference items) as input. The architecture includes multi-level embeddings and a multi-level branching design to produce level-wise matching facets; training uses a dual-mini-batch scheme and SGD with pretraining. Experiments on CiteULike, MovieLens+Posters, and Ciao show consistent gains over WMF and CDL, with particularly large improvements in out-of-matrix (cold-start item) predictions.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses an important practical problem (cold-start and better cross-domain matching) and proposes a simple, interpretable formulation: learnable encoders on both sides whose inner products predict matching.
- The idea of directly encoding users via a deep network that consumes user history (motivated by the linear relation from MF) is well motivated and practically useful: it naturally handles unseen items/users.
- The multi-level branching design and the observation that different layer representations are complementary are sensible and supported by ablation studies showing improved performance with branching.
- Experimental results on three real datasets (including explicit out-of-matrix evaluation) show substantial improvements over the reported baselines, especially for generalization to unseen items.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Limited comparison set: experiments compare mainly to WMF and CDL. Other contemporary baselines for implicit feedback and recent neural CF approaches (pairwise ranking methods, factorization machines, or neural collaborative filtering variants) are missing or not fully reported. The paper reports BPR results briefly on one dataset and claims similarity to WMF, but broader comparisons would strengthen claims of state-of-the-art.
- Loss choice and weighting: the objective (weighted squared loss on implicit data) and the choice of confidence weights cij are not described in detail in the excerpt; how sensitive are results to this choice? The paper mentions logistic loss degrades performance but more discussion/analysis of loss choices and class imbalance would help.
- Scalability and complexity: encoding a user requires feeding a high-dimensional sparse history vector ri to the user-network; practical memory and runtime implications (for very large item vocabularies) are not fully discussed. Also, training with dual mini-batches that load submatrices of R may be costly; complexity analysis or runtime comparisons are missing.
- Cold-start for users: while the method generalizes to unseen items (via item encoder), handling new users with no or very sparse history is not addressed; the approach depends on ri so true cold-start users (zero-history) remain problematic. This limitation should be clarified and discussed.
- Ablation and statistical rigor: while some design choices are ablated (multi-level, branching, noise), more exhaustive ablations (e.g. depth/width, embedding sizes, effect of pretraining) and statistical significance testing of reported gains would make the experimental claims stronger.
- Some training details are underspecified: the initialization/pretraining schedule, how the dual batches are sampled (and how sparsity/positive-negative coverage is ensured), regularization and hyperparameter search ranges are not fully described in the provided text.

Minor issues / clarity:
- Diagrams referenced (Figure 1) are not available in the text provided; the description is understandable but could be clearer with visuals.
- The paper should clarify how item features are preprocessed (e.g. bag-of-words, TF-IDF, CNN features) for each dataset in more detail.

Suggestions to improve the paper:
- Add comparisons to more recent or diverse baselines (pairwise ranking methods like BPR across datasets, neural CF, factorization machines, content-only baselines) and report significance tests.
- Provide a complexity/runtime analysis and report wall-clock training and inference times vs baselines, and memory use for large item vocabularies.
- Discuss limitations explicitly: users with no history, dependence on quality of item features, and when the method may not help (e.g. when item content is weakly related to preference).
- Expand ablation studies (pretraining vs training from scratch, branch depth/width, different losses, sensitivity to hyperparameters) and include more ranking metrics (e.g. NDCG) in addition to Recall@M.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid, practically-motivated paper that extends recommendation by jointly learning deep encoders for items and users and demonstrates strong empirical gains, particularly for cold-start items. The contribution is incremental rather than a radical theoretical advance, but the engineering and empirical aspects are valuable. With broader baselines, additional analysis of efficiency and limitations, and some clarification of training details, the paper would be substantially stronger. I recommend acceptance (score 7) contingent on addressing the experimental and clarity concerns above.
